cards.question: State the central idea of electron-pair repulsion theory.
cards.answer: Electron pairs around a central atom repel each other and arrange themselves as far apart as possible to minimise repulsion.
markPoints: electron pairs repel and get as far apart as possible
cards.explanation: Count ALL electron pairs (bonding + lone) around the central atom to predict the shape.

cards.question: State the order of repulsion strength between lone pairs and bonding pairs.
cards.answer: lone pair–lone pair > lone pair–bonding pair > bonding pair–bonding pair.
cards.explanation: Each lone pair reduces the bond angle by roughly 2.5°.

cards.question: State the shape and bond angle of a molecule of methane, CH₄.
cards.explanation: 4 bonding pairs, no lone pairs → tetrahedral, 109.5°.

cards.question: Explain the shape and bond angle of an ammonia molecule, NH₃.
cards.answer: N has 3 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair (4 electron pairs total). The lone pair repels more strongly than the bonding pairs, pushing the bonds closer together. The shape is pyramidal with a bond angle of 107°.
markPoints: lone pair repels more, reducing the angle
cards.explanation: Start from tetrahedral (4 pairs); the lone pair squeezes the angle from 109.5° to 107°.

cards.question: State the shape and bond angle of (i) CO₂ and (ii) SF₆.
cards.explanation: CO₂ has 2 bonding regions (double bonds count as one region); SF₆ has 6 bonding pairs.

cards.question: State the shape and bond angle of a water molecule, H₂O.
cards.explanation: 2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs; two lone pairs reduce the angle to 104.5°.
